metadata.title: Money market funds (~$7.9T) - the constant-NAV run vehicle that the bank lens misses, and the stablecoin analogy
metadata.frame: Money market funds are a ~$7.9T cash pool that sits OUTSIDE the FDIC bank lens, dominate the repo and Treasury-bill markets, and run by redemption (not credit). They are the institutional analogue of stablecoins - constant-value, redeemable-at-par, T-bill-backed, no FDIC. This block sizes the pool, the run history (2008 Reserve Primary, March 2020 prime run), the repo/ON-RRP plumbing, and the stablecoin parallel. Companion to [[macro-uncovered-risk-pools]] and [[macro-stablecoin-failures-manipulation]].
metadata.relevance: Adds the single largest non-bank cash pool to the uncovered-stack tally; links the bank/repo plumbing to the stablecoin rail; documents the post-RRP-drain reserve-scarcity backdrop that the AI-debt wall presses on through long rates.
metadata.sources: ICI weekly MMF assets (June 2026); SEC 2014 + 2023 Rule 2a-7 reform releases; Reserve Primary Fund history; Fed FEDS Note (prime-MMF investor base) + Boston Fed (MMLF); FRED ON-RRP series; Treasury refunding (T-bill supply); GENIUS Act text + Brookings; Wolf Street / NY Fed (2025-26 repo + SRF stress). Full URLs in the rendered .md.

size_composition.summary: A ~$7.9T pool that surged with the rate cycle; ~82% government, ~16% prime, ~2% tax-exempt.

points.detail: ~$7.87T for the week ended June 10, 2026 (record high); up from ~$7.0T in March 2025 and ~$4.5T at the 2022 rate-hike start. The pool roughly doubled in four years as front-end yields drew in cash.

Government MMFs ~$6.5T (~82-83%), prime MMFs ~$1.23T (~16%; institutional prime only ~$241B, retail prime ~$989B), tax-exempt/muni ~$145B (~2%). The largest segment - government funds - is the least reform-constrained.
Retail ~$3.10T; institutional ~$4.78T (June 2026). Institutional cash is the faster-moving, more run-prone tranche.

reforms.summary: Two reform waves shifted risk from prime to government funds without removing run dynamics.

points.detail: Adopted July 2014 (effective Oct 2016): institutional prime/muni funds float their NAV; boards got discretionary fees/gates. Result: ~$1T+ migrated from prime to government funds, hollowing out the prime segment.

Adopted July 2023 (amended Rule 2a-7): removed redemption gates; added a mandatory liquidity fee for institutional prime/muni when daily net redemptions exceed 5% of NAV; raised daily liquid assets to 25% and weekly to 50%.

Government MMFs (~82% of the industry) keep a stable $1.00 NAV with no fees/gates - and government-fund redemptions, not credit losses, were the March-2020 dash-for-cash channel. Reform shifted, it did not eliminate, the systemic exposure.

run_history.summary: Two precedents prove the run mechanism; both required a Treasury/Fed backstop.

points.detail: Sept 16, 2008: the $62.6B Reserve Primary Fund marked its $785M Lehman holding to zero, NAV fell to $0.97; >$40B of redemptions in two days. Treasury guaranteed MMFs (Sept 19) via the ~$50B Exchange Stabilization Fund; the Fed launched the AMLF.

Mar 2-23, 2020: prime MMFs lost ~$125B (~11% of the segment) in under three weeks (public institutional prime outflows peaked ~35%) - a pure liquidity run with no credit default. The Fed's MMLF (announced Mar 18, $10B Treasury credit protection) drew ~$53B and halted it.

repo_treasury_plumbing.summary: MMFs are the dominant cash supplier to repo and the largest marginal T-bill buyer; the RRP shock-absorber is now drained.

points.detail: The Fed's reverse-repo facility peaked >$2.5T (Dec 2022) as MMFs parked cash; it drained to ~$22B by early Sept 2025 as funds rotated into higher-yielding T-bills and private repo. The post-pandemic excess-liquidity buffer is effectively exhausted.

MMFs hold roughly ~40% of outstanding T-bills - the largest marginal buyer - while the bill market absorbs a deficit-driven issuance surge (net bill issuance ~$475B Q3-2025, ~$416B Q1-2026; FY2025 deficit ~$1.75T). A redemption wave forces bill liquidation into a thin, supply-heavy market.
points.grade: contested (40% share widely cited, approximate) + fact (issuance)

points.detail: Year-end Dec 31, 2025: some repo prints hit ~4.0%; the Fed's Standing Repo Facility was tapped ~$74.6B (highest since COVID) before reversing to ~zero by Jan 2026. QT ended Dec 2025 and the Fed began reserve-management T-bill purchases - the first real test of post-RRP-drain plumbing.

stablecoin_analogy.summary: Stablecoins are unregulated constant-NAV money funds - same collateral pool, weaker backstop.

points.detail: Major fiat stablecoins are T-bill/repo-backed, redeemable at par, run-prone, with NO FDIC insurance and no stable-NAV legal backstop - functionally a constant-NAV money fund without Rule 2a-7 protections. USDC depegged to ~$0.87 in March 2023 on SVB-deposit fears, demonstrating the run dynamic.

The GENIUS Act (enacted July 2025) requires >=1:1 reserves in cash, insured deposits, short T-bills, T-bill repo, and government MMFs, but exempts issuers from bank capital standards and gives holders no FDIC coverage. Issuers bought ~$109B of T-bills (2025) to meet the rule - putting stablecoins and MMFs into the SAME bill-and-repo collateral pool, so a run in either forces the same fire-sale channel.

synthesis.summary: The largest uncovered cash pool, structurally run-prone, now without its RRP cushion.

points.detail: At ~$7.9T, MMFs are the single biggest non-bank cash pool outside the FDIC lens, the dominant repo lender, and the largest marginal T-bill holder - and the RRP buffer that absorbed shocks is gone. The same long-rate move that re-opens the bank HTM hole ([[macro-bank-htm-marks]]) and prices the other self-marked numbers also drives MMF redemptions and stablecoin redemptions into one shared bill/repo market.

non_claims: No claim an MMF is currently breaking the buck - the point is structural run-proneness plus a drained RRP cushion, not a present failure.
The ~40% MMF share of T-bills and the ~$109B stablecoin T-bill figure are widely cited but approximate (graded contested); exact shares live in OFR/Fed primary data.
The stablecoin-MMF link is a structural analogy and a shared-collateral channel, not a claim that stablecoins are MMFs in law.
Overlay edges are EXCLUDED from the SCC / Z3 / TLA+ proofs.

edges.raw_instrument: ~$7.9T pool; dominant cash supplier to repo; ON-RRP drained from >$2.5T to ~$22B, removing the shock absorber so funding stress now hits SOFR directly
edges.note: OVERLAY: cash-supply plumbing; excluded from proofs

edges.raw_instrument: MMFs hold ~40% of outstanding T-bills - largest marginal buyer absorbing the deficit-driven supply surge; a redemption wave forces bill liquidation
edges.note: OVERLAY: bill-market concentration; excluded from proofs

edges.raw_instrument: GENIUS-mandated T-bill/repo reserves (~$109B bought 2025) put stablecoins into the same collateral pool as MMFs - a shared fire-sale channel on a run
edges.note: OVERLAY: shared-collateral co-dependence; excluded from proofs

edges.raw_instrument: MMFs compete with bank deposits for cash; rate-driven flows into MMFs drain deposit funding (the SVB-era deposit-flight channel)
edges.note: OVERLAY: deposit competition; excluded from proofs

limits: Sizes the ~$7.9T MMF pool (June 2026 ICI): ~82% government / ~16% prime / ~2% tax-exempt; documents the 2014/2023 Rule 2a-7 reforms; the 2008 Reserve Primary and March-2020 prime-run precedents (each backstopped); the repo/ON-RRP plumbing (RRP drained >$2.5T -> ~$22B) and ~40% T-bill share; the 2025-26 year-end repo/SRF stress; and the stablecoin analogy (constant-NAV, T-bill-backed, no FDIC; ~$109B GENIUS-driven bill buying into the shared collateral pool). Overlay edges excluded from proofs. Several shares (40% T-bills, $109B stablecoin bills) are approximate.

checks.result: ~$7.87T total (ICI, wk of Jun 10 2026); ~$6.5T gov / ~$1.23T prime / ~$145B tax-exempt.
2014 floating-NAV institutional prime (effective 2016); 2023 removed gates, added 5%-redemption liquidity fee, raised liquidity minimums (SEC).
2008 Reserve Primary broke the buck (NAV $0.97, >$40B out); March-2020 prime lost ~$125B/~11%, MMLF drew ~$53B.
ON-RRP >$2.5T (Dec 2022) -> ~$22B (Sept 2025); MMFs ~40% of T-bills; Dec-2025 SRF ~$74.6B, year-end repo ~4.0%.
GENIUS Act July 2025 reserve rules; issuers bought ~$109B T-bills (2025); USDC depeg to $0.87 (Mar 2023).
